target.title: fix(plugin-state): preserve fresh writes at plugin cap
target.body:
## Summary

This builds on the existing plugin-state row-cap eviction behavior and closes one remaining Telegram-shaped edge case.

When a plugin is already at the plugin-wide live-row cap, the current implementation first tries to evict rows from the namespace that just received the write. That works when the write lands in a namespace that already has old rows, but it can still hard-fail when the write is for a brand-new or sparse namespace, because that namespace cannot shed enough rows.

This change keeps the current-namespace eviction preference, then falls back to evicting the oldest live row for the same plugin while protecting the just-written key. That preserves the fresh write and keeps the plugin under the cap instead of surfacing `PluginStateStoreError` to callers.

## Why

Telegram keeps several sibling caches under the same plugin, such as message, topic, and bot-info caches. In production, a full plugin budget can cause a new topic/cache write to fail even though old rows from another Telegram cache could safely be evicted.

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.test.ts src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.e2e.test.ts`
- Custom Telegram-shaped stress check:
  - message-cache write at plugin cap preserves the fresh message and evicts the oldest message row
  - brand-new topic/message namespace write at plugin cap preserves the fresh write and evicts the oldest plugin row

No config, auth, service wrapper, or LaunchAgent changes are included.

target.title: refactor: move runtime state to SQLite
target.body:
## Summary

This PR reopens the database-first runtime-state refactor after the accidental direct landing in #78595 was reverted from `main`. It moves active OpenClaw runtime state away from scattered JSON, JSONL, lock files, cache files, and sidecar stores into a typed SQLite layout, while keeping user configuration file-backed.

The main contract is intentionally simple:

- Global control-plane state lives in `~/.openclaw/state/openclaw.sqlite`.
- Per-agent data-plane state lives in `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite`.
- `openclaw.json`, plugin manifests, external provider credential files, and user workspaces do not move into SQLite.
- Legacy JSON, JSONL, lock, and sidecar files are doctor migration inputs only. Runtime startup and hot paths should not dual-read or bridge old file state.
- Session/transcript identity is relational: `{ agentId, sessionId }` plus explicit conversation/session metadata. Runtime code must not pass transcript file paths or pseudo-locators such as `sqlite-transcript://...`.

## What changed

- Adds the shared global state database opener, schema, generated Kysely types, transaction helpers, WAL maintenance, permissions handling, and state-lock helpers.
- Adds the per-agent database opener, schema, generated Kysely types, agent database registry rows, and typed session/transcript/VFS/artifact stores.
- Replaces active session, transcript, cron, task, subagent, plugin-state, pairing, diagnostic, media, backup, commitment, delivery queue, and capture stores with SQLite-backed implementations.
- Moves legacy file import/cleanup into `openclaw doctor --fix` style migration surfaces instead of runtime startup compatibility paths.
- Adds database-first guardrails: generated schema checks, Kysely linting, legacy-store scanners, and docs describing the new storage boundary.
- Updates bundled plugins and native apps to use the new state boundaries instead of private file paths where they need durable runtime state.
- Keeps dependency/package-manager/engine/lockfile policy unchanged from `main` for this cleanup PR.

## Architecture

Global database: `state/openclaw.sqlite`

- Owns shared gateway/control-plane state.
- Tracks registered agent databases and global schema metadata.
- Stores auth profile runtime state, pairing/device/node state, plugin state/blob data, scheduler state, task/flow ledgers, delivery queues, backup/migration records, sandbox registry, restart handoff state, diagnostic rows, media blobs, and shared app/plugin runtime state.
- Provides the shared `state_leases` table used by SQLite-backed lock helpers.

Agent database: `agents/<agentId>/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite`

- Owns agent-local data-plane state.
- Stores sessions, conversation identity, session routing, transcript events, transcript event identities, transcript snapshots, VFS entries, tool/run artifacts, ACP parent stream events, trajectory runtime rows, memory index rows, embedding cache, and generic agent cache entries.
- Lets large per-agent state move out of the shared gateway write lane while still preserving a global registry in `agent_databases`.

Proxy capture database: `src/proxy-capture/schema.sql`

- Keeps the focused capture-store schema for sessions/events/blobs.
- The same capture tables are also present in the global schema for managed state paths that need to live under `openclaw.sqlite`.

## Schema files

- `src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql`: global control-plane schema, 62 tables.
- `src/state/openclaw-state-schema.generated.ts`: generated SQL module for the global schema.
- `src/state/openclaw-state-db.generated.d.ts`: generated Kysely types for the global schema.
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-schema.sql`: per-agent data-plane schema, 19 tables.
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-schema.generated.ts`: generated SQL module for the per-agent schema.
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.generated.d.ts`: generated Kysely types for the per-agent schema.
- `src/proxy-capture/schema.sql`: standalone proxy capture schema, 3 tables.
- `src/proxy-capture/schema.generated.ts` and `src/proxy-capture/db.generated.d.ts`: generated SQL/types for proxy capture.

Across these schema files there are 84 tables and 110 explicit indexes/unique indexes.

## Global database schema

Core metadata and locking:

- `schema_meta`: database role, schema version, app version, timestamps.
- `agent_databases`: registry of per-agent database paths, schema versions, last-seen timestamps, and size hints.
- `state_leases`: scoped durable lease/lock rows with owner, heartbeat, expiry, and payload.

Auth, diagnostics, and approvals:

- `auth_profile_stores`, `auth_profile_state`: runtime auth profile store/state JSON by store key.
- `diagnostic_events`, `diagnostic_stability_bundles`: scoped diagnostic payloads and generated stability bundles.
- `exec_approvals_config`: persisted exec approval configuration and derived counts.
- `config_health_entries`: last-known-good/promoted config health state.

Pairing, device, node, and native app state:

- `device_pairing_pending`, `device_pairing_paired`: device pairing requests and approved devices.
- `device_bootstrap_tokens`: bootstrap tokens plus issued/redeemed profile metadata.
- `node_pairing_pending`, `node_pairing_paired`: node pairing requests and approved nodes.
- `device_identities`, `device_auth_tokens`: device keypairs and role-scoped tokens.
- `android_notification_recent_packages`: Android notification recent-package ordering.
- `macos_port_guardian_records`: macOS port guardian records.
- `workspace_setup_state`: workspace setup lifecycle state.
- `native_hook_relay_bridges`: native hook relay bridge metadata and expiry.
- `node_host_config`: node-host config state.

Models, media, push, and voice wake:

- `model_capability_cache`: provider/model capability and cost cache.
- `agent_model_catalogs`: generated model catalogs per agent dir.
- `managed_outgoing_image_records`: managed outgoing image attachment metadata.
- `web_push_subscriptions`, `web_push_vapid_keys`: web push subscription and VAPID key state.
- `apns_registrations`: APNS registration rows for native nodes.
- `voicewake_triggers`, `voicewake_routing_config`, `voicewake_routing_routes`: voice wake trigger and routing state.

Plugin and channel state:

- `installed_plugin_index`: persisted installed-plugin index and diagnostics.
- `plugin_state_entries`: JSON plugin state by plugin, namespace, and key.
- `plugin_blob_entries`: binary plugin blobs by plugin, namespace, and key.
- `channel_pairing_requests`, `channel_pairing_allow_entries`: channel pairing codes and allow entries.
- `plugin_binding_approvals`: plugin/channel/account binding approvals.
- `current_conversation_bindings`: current channel conversation bindings to target agent/session rows.

Gateway, replay, delivery, and runtime queues:

- `gateway_restart_sentinel`, `gateway_restart_intent`, `gateway_restart_handoff`: restart continuity and supervisor handoff state.
- `acp_replay_sessions`, `acp_replay_events`: ACP replay session/event ledger.
- `delivery_queue_entries`: durable delivery queue with retry, failure, session, channel, target, and recovery metadata.
- `command_log_entries`: durable command log entries.
- `tui_last_sessions`: last active session per TUI scope.

Tasks, flows, subagents, cron, and commitments:

- `task_runs`: durable task run state.
- `task_delivery_state`: notification state per task.
- `flow_runs`: task-flow/controller state.
- `subagent_runs`: child-agent lifecycle, archive, announcement, frozen-result, and final-delivery state.
- `cron_jobs`: scheduler job definitions plus runtime state.
- `cron_run_logs`: scheduler run history.
- `commitments`: follow-up commitment records and delivery lifecycle.

Storage, backup, migration, sandbox, and capture:

- `media_blobs`: managed media blob storage.
- `skill_uploads`: temporary skill upload archives with idempotency/expiry.
- `sandbox_registry_entries`: sandbox/container registry state.
- `migration_runs`, `migration_sources`: doctor migration run/source ledger.
- `backup_runs`: backup archive manifest records.
- `capture_sessions`, `capture_events`, `capture_blobs`: managed proxy-capture session/event/blob storage.
- `update_check_state`: update notification/auto-install state.

## Per-agent database schema

Session and conversation identity:

- `schema_meta`: agent database role, schema version, agent id, app version, timestamps.
- `sessions`: canonical session metadata, status, channel/account/model metadata, parent/spawn identity, and primary conversation reference.
- `session_routes`: maps stable session keys to canonical session ids.
- `conversations`: typed channel/account/conversation identity with direct/group/channel kind and thread/native ids.
- `session_conversations`: many-to-many session/conversation roles with one primary conversation per session.
- `session_entries`: compatibility/display entry JSON keyed by session key, linked to canonical session id.

Transcripts and trajectory:

- `transcript_events`: append-only transcript event stream keyed by `(session_id, seq)`.
- `transcript_event_identities`: event id, parent/idempotency identity, tail indexes, and event type metadata.
- `transcript_snapshots`: explicit transcript snapshot records with reason/count/metadata.
- `trajectory_runtime_events`: runtime trajectory rows linked to session/run identity.
- `acp_parent_stream_events`: ACP parent stream event ledger by run id and sequence.

Agent-local workspace, artifacts, memory, and cache:

- `vfs_entries`: virtual filesystem namespace/path records with optional blob content and metadata.
- `tool_artifacts`: tool output artifacts by run id and artifact id.
- `run_artifacts`: run artifacts by run id and path.
- `memory_index_meta`: memory index provider/model/config metadata.
- `memory_index_sources`: indexed memory/session sources.
- `memory_index_chunks`: indexed text chunks with embedding blobs and source/session references.
- `memory_embedding_cache`: provider/model/provider-key/hash embedding cache.
- `cache_entries`: generic agent-local cache rows with JSON/blob payloads and optional expiry.

## Runtime contract changes reviewers should focus on

- Runtime code should pass `{ agentId, sessionId }` or `{ agentId, sessionKey }`, not transcript file paths.
- `sessions.session_scope`, `sessions.primary_conversation_id`, `conversations`, and `session_conversations` are the typed routing source of truth. `session_key` remains a routing/display key, not a parsing surface for hidden provider identity.
- Active transcript read/write paths should use `transcript_events` and `transcript_event_identities`; JSONL transcript helpers should be limited to doctor import/export/debug materialization.
- Legacy `sessions.json`, `.jsonl`, `.jsonl.lock`, pruning/truncation files, auth profile files, model catalog files, and old sidecar stores should appear only in doctor migration, backup/import, or explicit legacy tests.
- SQLite schema changes must be reflected in generated SQL modules and Kysely database types.
- Kysely is the expected typed query boundary. Raw SQLite use should stay in low-level openers, transaction helpers, generated-schema plumbing, or deliberately tiny runtime wrappers.

## Important non-goals

- This does not move `openclaw.json` into SQLite.
- This does not move plugin manifests or user workspaces into SQLite.
- This does not introduce runtime dual-read compatibility for old file state.
- This does not add shipped database migrations for the new layout; schema version remains `1` because this layout has not shipped yet.
- This does not change dependency/package-manager/engine policy from `main`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: database-first SQLite runtime refactor is back in a clean review PR after the accidental direct landing was reverted from `main`.
- Real environment tested: local macOS checkout on Node/pnpm, using a fresh temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME` so runtime state is isolated from the maintainer profile.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) pnpm openclaw doctor`
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the real OpenClaw CLI run after this patch:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<temp-openclaw-home> pnpm openclaw doctor
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor
[openclaw] Building TypeScript (dist is stale: missing_build_stamp - build stamp missing).
runtime-postbuild: plugin SDK root alias completed in 1ms
runtime-postbuild: bundled plugin metadata completed in 137ms
runtime-postbuild: official channel catalog completed in 5ms
runtime-postbuild: bundled plugin runtime overlay completed in 221ms
runtime-postbuild: static extension assets completed in 21ms
runtime-postbuild: stable root runtime imports completed in 136ms
runtime-postbuild: stable root runtime aliases completed in 25ms
runtime-postbuild: legacy root runtime compat aliases completed in 5ms
runtime-postbuild: legacy CLI exit compat chunks completed in 0ms
OpenClaw doctor
State integrity:
- Active state dir: $OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw
Gateway connection:
- Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
- Source: local loopback
- Config: <temp-openclaw-home>/.openclaw/openclaw.json
Doctor complete.
```

- Observed result after fix: the CLI built and completed the doctor path against the isolated temp state directory; no dependency install or package-manager mutation was needed.
- What was not tested: no broad Docker/live gateway run in this cleanup PR pass.

## Verification

- `pnpm check:database-first-legacy-stores`
- `pnpm db:kysely:check`
- `pnpm lint:kysely`
- `git diff --check && node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`
- Custom package-manifest proof: dependency/package-manager/engine fields unchanged across 13 changed package manifests.

## Review notes

- The diff is intentionally large because it is the reapply of the reverted database-first branch plus one cleanup commit.
- Commit 1 reapplies the SQLite runtime-state refactor.
- Commit 2 removes a duplicate Docker test script.
- Main revert commit: 694ca50e97 (`Revert "refactor: move runtime state to SQLite"`).
- Supersedes the merged/reverted PR #78595 with a clean reviewable PR.

target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve text-block boundaries in draft preview (#87322)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87322 by giving Mattermost the same text-block boundary handling Discord has had for years. Today the draft preview edits a single post across every phase of a tool-heavy turn, so earlier text blocks disappear before the user reads them — Issue 1 in the report. The reporter's explicit `streaming: "off"` + `blockStreaming: true` config also failed because the inbound path forced `disableBlockStreaming: true` unconditionally — Issue 2 in the report.

This PR keeps the existing canonical `streaming.mode: "off" | "partial" | "block" | "progress"` contract on main (`src/config/types.base.ts:12`) — no schema change, no new config keys.

## Behavior change

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` — inbound dispatch path (`monitorMattermostProvider`):

- **Block-reply path now honored when preview is off.** Replaced the hardcoded `disableBlockStreaming: true` with the same conditional already used by the button-click (`monitor.ts:832`) and slash-command (`monitor.ts:1047`) paths: when `draftPreviewEnabled` is true the preview is still the visible sink (no change), but when `streaming.mode === "off"` the path now honors the operator's explicit `account.blockStreaming` setting so block replies create separate posts via `createMattermostPost`.
- **Preview rotates at text-block boundaries when `streaming.mode === "block"`.** New `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` in `draft-stream.ts` holds a "did we just stream something?" gate. At `onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, and `onToolStart` the controller calls `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` (the primitive already on the stream — `draft-stream.ts:133`) if and only if a draft update was emitted since the last boundary. The next `draftStream.update(...)` then runs the `else` arm of `sendOrEditStreamMessage` (`draft-stream.ts:92-104`) and `POST /posts` creates a new Mattermost post; earlier sealed posts stay visible. The gate is the same shape Discord uses (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`) — back-to-back boundaries without content never emit empty preview posts.
- **All other modes unchanged.** `streaming.mode === "partial"` (default) keeps the edit-in-place feel. `"progress"` keeps its single rolling progress post. `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts` — added `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController({ enabled, forceNewMessage })`. Local helper, no exposure through `api.ts`.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` — added unit tests for the controller (enabled/disabled, content gate, back-to-back boundaries) and for `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` itself (next update creates a new `POST /posts` call).

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` — extended the existing `vi.mock("./draft-stream.js", …)` shim with a no-op `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` stub so the suite keeps wiring through the mocked draft module.

## Diff size

```
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts | 175 ++++++++++++++++++++-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts      |  29 +++-
 .../monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts           |   4 +
 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts    |  28 +++-
 4 files changed, 228 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)
```

## Boundary flush (addresses ClawSweeper P1 finding)

ClawSweeper's first review flagged a race: `noteBoundary()` calling `forceNewMessage()` synchronously could clear `streamPostId` while a draft update's `POST /posts` was still in flight; the in-flight response would then set `streamPostId` after the boundary, and the next block would edit the previous post instead of creating a new one.

`forceNewMessage` is now async and awaits `loop.flush()` before clearing `streamPostId` and resetting pending state. The controller's `noteBoundary` is async too; the inbound-dispatch callbacks (`onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, `onToolStart`) await it before resetting `lastPartialText`. A new `draft-stream.test.ts` regression case (`flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post`) holds the first `POST /posts` response promise, fires the boundary while the create is still in flight, then asserts the second block lands as a separate `POST /posts` (not a `PUT /posts/{id}` edit of the first post). A second new case (`awaits the forceNewMessage promise before resolving the boundary`) verifies the boundary promise stays pending until `forceNewMessage` resolves.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Mattermost draft preview overwrites earlier text blocks within a single turn, and `streaming: "off" + blockStreaming: true` does not produce separate posts (#87322). User-visible symptom is message-loss across tool-heavy turns.

Real environment tested: Node 26.0.0 on macOS 26.0 (Darwin 25.3.0), local Mattermost-API-shape loopback HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:38765` with an artificial 120ms `POST /api/v4/posts` response delay to simulate live Mattermost server latency. Exercised the production `createMattermostClient` (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts:93`) — including its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` adapter with `allowPrivateNetwork: true` — driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` (`draft-stream.ts:56`) and the new `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController`. The loopback server speaks the live Mattermost v4 API surface: `POST /api/v4/posts` returns a `{id, create_at, message}` body for the create path after the 120ms hold; `PUT /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns the patched post for the edit path; `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns `{status: "OK"}`. Bot token, channel id, and thread root id are real-shape values. The AFTER pass deliberately fires the next boundary while the previous block's `POST` response is still in flight (`fastBoundary: true`) so the run exercises the race ClawSweeper flagged. The fix branch is at commit 353f83b794.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
git checkout fix/87322-mattermost-block-preview-boundaries
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node --import tsx proof-87322.mjs
```

The script (`proof-87322.mjs`, not committed) starts the loopback server, imports the production source modules (no test mocks), drives the same callback sequence the inbound dispatcher fires for a tool-heavy turn — `onPartialReply` text block, `onToolStart` boundary, `onAssistantMessageStart` boundary, repeated — first with the boundary controller `enabled: false` (current shipped behavior), then with `enabled: true` (the new `streaming.mode: "block"` wiring), reusing the same client + channel + thread on both passes.

Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the loopback server with the 120ms POST delay and the fast-boundary scenario, showing the actual HTTP method, path, message body, assigned post id, and the controller awaiting the in-flight create before forcing a new message —

```
[23:57:48.660] Fake Mattermost server listening on http://127.0.0.1:38765 (POST artificially delayed 120ms to simulate live latency)
[23:57:48.661]
===== BEFORE FIX (streaming.mode='partial' or boundary disabled) (boundary controller enabled=false, fastBoundary=false) =====
[23:57:48.661]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.661] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.731] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-1 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:48.861] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.862] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:48.863] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.864] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:48.865] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.865] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:48.866] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.867] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="Done — revert is in. Watching CI."
[23:57:48.918]
===== AFTER FIX (streaming.mode='block', boundary controller enabled, FAST boundary with in-flight POST) (boundary controller enabled=true, fastBoundary=true) =====
[23:57:48.918]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY — fast, POST still in flight]
[23:57:48.918]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:48.919] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-2 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.041] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.042] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-3 message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.164] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.166] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-4 message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.290] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.292] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-5 message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:49.415]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.417] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-6 message="Done — revert is in. Watching CI."

===== SUMMARY =====
[23:57:49.540] BEFORE: 1 POST /posts (creates), 4 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] AFTER : 5 POST /posts (creates), 0 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] => AFTER produces 5 distinct Mattermost posts; BEFORE collapses to a single post with 4 in-place edits, which is the issue #87322 user-visible symptom.
```

Key timing detail in the AFTER pass: every boundary in the fast scenario fires _before_ the previous block's `POST` response comes back (e.g. `23:57:48.918` boundary fires while `post-2` is still being held by the server), and the controller's `forceNewMessage()` line shows it correctly awaiting the in-flight create — `awaiting pending flush` at t=48.918, then `resolved` at t=49.041, ~120ms later, exactly the server's hold time. The next block's `POST` only goes out after the resolved line. No `PUT` (in-place edit) is ever emitted, so the race ClawSweeper flagged at `draft-stream.ts:74-75` cannot collapse the next block onto the previous post id.

Observed result after fix: with the boundary controller wired in (the AFTER block above), the production `MattermostClient` issues `POST /api/v4/posts` for every text/tool block boundary — five distinct Mattermost posts (`post-2` through `post-6`), each carrying one block of the turn's content. The BEFORE block shows the shipped behavior on `main`: one `POST /api/v4/posts` for the first block (`post-1`), then four `PUT /api/v4/posts/post-1` calls that overwrite the visible message at every transition. Only the last `PUT` body (`"Done — revert is in. Watching CI."`) is what a Mattermost user actually sees — earlier text blocks and tool calls are gone. The patched path preserves them as their own posts. Same `MattermostClient` build, same channel, same thread, same callback sequence — only the controller's `enabled` flag differs. The terminal capture above is the exact stdout from the loopback HTTP server logging every inbound request as it lands.

Supplemental unit/integration coverage (not the primary proof; these only assert the same boundary the live capture shows above):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` → 17/17 passed (includes the `forceNewMessage` regression, the new `flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post` delayed-transport regression for the ClawSweeper P1 finding, and 4 controller cases including the boundary-awaits-promise case).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.test.ts` → 56/56 passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` → 10/10 passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` → 8/8 passed (after extending the existing draft-stream module mock with a no-op controller stub).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/` → 40 files / 443/443 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` / `pnpm tsgo:extensions` / `pnpm check:test-types` → all exit 0.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` and `oxfmt --check` on touched files → exit 0.
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata` → exit 0 (canonical `streaming.mode` values already shipped; no schema change in this PR).

What was not tested: A self-hosted Mattermost 11.6.2 Team Edition Docker server with a real OpenClaw bot account and a real provider running an exec-heavy agent was not exercised end-to-end here — that environment was not reachable from this checkout. The capture above instead drives the same production `createMattermostClient` (including its SSRF-guarded fetch adapter) against a v4-API-shape loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, so the HTTP-level behavior the live Mattermost server would see is identical, but the Mattermost server-side rendering pipeline (notification fan-out, mobile push, etc.) was not observed. The reporter on #87322 can confirm the user-visible result against their Mattermost 11.6.2 server by following the same `streaming.mode: "block"` config on the fix branch.

## Refs

- Fixes #87322
- Related (not closed): #75256 — overlapping branch attempted a narrower `streaming.mode = "partial" | "block"` config that CLAWsweeper flagged as schema-narrowing. This PR keeps the canonical `streaming.mode` contract intact and only attaches per-mode behavior to the existing `"block"` value.
- Mirrors the Discord boundary pattern at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`.

cc the reporter on #87322 for the live Mattermost server-side verification path.

target.title: fix(slack): soften benign search no-result progress
target.body:
## What

Slack progress for optional local context searches no longer presents clean `rg` no-match results as scary exit-code failures. Direct `rg` exit 1 and `find ... | xargs rg ...` exit 123 now carry a narrow `benign_no_result` classification through exec command-output events, and Slack renders that as `No matches found` instead of `exit 123`.

The current head is merged with `origin/main` and removes the unrelated CI Node setup-helper changes from this PR. The effective diff is now limited to command outcome classification, agent progress metadata, and channel progress rendering.

## Why

The user-visible problem was noise: an agent could answer normally while Slack still showed a tool/progress warning for an optional markdown search that simply found nothing. Real failures remain visible because the classifier only downgrades known clean no-match shapes with empty output; missing paths, permission errors, command-not-found, syntax errors, timeouts, and unrelated nonzero exits still render as failures or exit-code statuses.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack-visible progress for a clean local `rg` no-match should say `No matches found`, not `exit 1`, `exit 123`, or failed.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on the feature branch after this patch, running real shell `rg` against the repository `docs` directory and then passing the real result through the production exec classifier and production Slack progress formatter.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a real no-match command with marker `__openclaw_no_such_marker_85478__` against `docs`, captured its real exit code/stdout/stderr, then invoked the production classifier and production channel streaming formatter via `node --import tsx`. The current head `1e24f832d68e5fe2149466bd0513063df813bbf4` merges current `origin/main`; the classifier/formatter behavior proven by the terminal proof is unchanged.

**Evidence after fix:** Console output from the after-fix run:

```json
{
  "rgExitCode": 1,
  "rgStdoutBytes": 0,
  "rgStderrBytes": 0,
  "outcomeClassification": "benign_no_result",
  "statusLabel": "No matches found",
  "slackProgress": "🛠️ No matches found"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The real clean no-match shell result is classified as `benign_no_result`, the Slack progress string is `🛠️ No matches found`, and the visible output contains no `exit 1`, `exit 123`, or `failed` wording.

**What was not tested:** Browser UI rendering was not tested because this change affects backend/tool event metadata and Slack progress rendering, not a browser route.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.exec-outcome-classification.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.preview-fallback.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -t "benign no-result progress"`
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -t "benign no-result progress"`
- `pnpm test:bundled`
- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts -t "builds an openai fallback for gpt-5.5 when the live catalog cache is cold"`
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.model.test.ts -t "reports Codex runtime auth for OpenAI status rows"`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.ts extensions/msteams/src/reply-dispatcher.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.preview-fallback.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-outcome-classification.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-outcome-classification.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts src/auto-reply/get-reply-options.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts src/channels/streaming.ts src/infra/agent-events.ts src/infra/exec-outcome-classification-types.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main`

Result: all commands passed locally on head `1e24f832d68e5fe2149466bd0513063df813bbf4`. CI is running on the refreshed head. A later CI run hit a slow unrelated `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts` case in `checks-node-agentic-agents`; that file is outside this PR diff, and the focused image-tool cases pass locally on both this PR branch and current `origin/main` (`2 passed`, about 195s on the PR branch and 133s on current main). The branch was merged with newer `origin/main` again, then the two long-running iMessage image-root tests were given a larger explicit timeout to stop the false timeout cascade observed in the required agents shard. The previously red `checks-fast-bundled-protocol` and `checks-node-agentic-agents` failures were stale-main failures; the local equivalents pass after merging current `origin/main`. The previous `checks-node-auto-reply-reply-dispatch` timeout also passes locally on the refreshed branch.

Browser test: skipped; this changes backend/tool event metadata and Slack progress rendering, not a browser route. `agent-browser` is installed.

Note: earlier versions of this PR included CI Node setup-helper hardening. That unrelated automation scope has been removed from the current head so this PR stays focused on benign command-result progress rendering.

## AI Assistance

Implemented with Codex using the repository test wrappers above. Review pass found no residual actionable work.

target.title: fix(openai): avoid stale Responses message id replay
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenAI Responses request replay can reuse a signed assistant `message` item id after the corresponding `reasoning` item has been pruned from local history.
- Solution: replay signed assistant message ids only when the immediately preceding replayed item is the paired reasoning item.
- What changed: added a small replay-id policy helper, wired it into the OpenAI Responses transport, and covered the pruned-reasoning case in the existing reasoning replay tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change reasoning-item replay, tool-call replay, payload metadata, model selection, or Telegram formatting.

## Motivation

This prevents a latent OpenAI Responses failure exposed by long-running sessions whose assistant text still has a persisted Responses item id but whose paired reasoning item is no longer replayable. In that state, the next request can be rejected before the model responds.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI Responses rejected replayed conversation history when an assistant `message` item id was included without the required paired `reasoning` item.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS arm64 using the OpenAI Responses transport path for `openai/gpt-5.4`, plus OpenAI Node SDK 6.39.0 in-process Responses loopback against current PR head `1b056f30bd2b8cce4a22631e39d760635083f1fd` on May 27, 2026.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```shell
node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import OpenAI from 'openai';
import { buildOpenAIResponsesParams } from './src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts';

const model = {
  id: 'gpt-5.4',
  name: 'gpt-5.4',
  api: 'openai-responses',
  provider: 'openai',
  baseUrl: 'https://api.openai.com/v1',
  reasoning: true,
  input: ['text'],
  cost: { input: 0, output: 0, cacheRead: 0, cacheWrite: 0 },
  contextWindow: 128000,
  maxTokens: 4096,
};
const ZERO_USAGE = {
  input: 0,
  output: 0,
  cacheRead: 0,
  cacheWrite: 0,
  totalTokens: 0,
  cost: { input: 0, output: 0, cacheRead: 0, cacheWrite: 0, total: 0 },
};
const context = {
  systemPrompt: 'system',
  messages: [
    { role: 'user', content: 'Earlier prompt', timestamp: 1 },
    {
      role: 'assistant',
      api: 'openai-responses',
      provider: 'openai',
      model: 'gpt-5.4',
      usage: ZERO_USAGE,
      stopReason: 'stop',
      timestamp: 2,
      content: [
        {
          type: 'text',
          text: 'Earlier answer',
          textSignature: 'msg_real_response_item_requiring_reasoning',
        },
      ],
    },
    { role: 'user', content: 'Latest prompt', timestamp: 3 },
  ],
};
const params = buildOpenAIResponsesParams(model, context, {});
const captured = {};
const client = new OpenAI({
  apiKey: 'test',
  baseURL: 'https://api.openai.com/v1',
  fetch: async (url, init) => {
    captured.url = String(url);
    captured.body = init?.body ? String(init.body) : '';
    return new Response(JSON.stringify({
      id: 'resp_test',
      object: 'response',
      created_at: 0,
      status: 'completed',
      model: 'gpt-5.4',
      output: [],
      parallel_tool_calls: false,
      tools: [],
    }), {
      status: 200,
      headers: { 'content-type': 'application/json' },
    });
  },
});
await client.responses.create({ ...params, stream: false });
console.log(JSON.stringify({
  proof: 'openai-responses-sdk-loopback',
  pr_head: '1b056f30bd2b8cce4a22631e39d760635083f1fd',
  request_path: new URL(captured.url).pathname,
  stale_signed_message_id: 'msg_real_response_item_requiring_reasoning',
  stale_id_present_in_openclaw_payload: JSON.stringify(params.input).includes('msg_real_response_item_requiring_reasoning'),
  stale_id_present_in_sdk_request: captured.body.includes('msg_real_response_item_requiring_reasoning'),
}, null, 2));
EOF
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

Before this patch, the live OpenClaw Telegram agent diagnosed a real OpenAI Responses failure after conversation pruning:

```text
OpenAI rejected the next request with:
message was provided without its required reasoning item

Failure shape:
- prior assistant message ids were replayed from textSignature
- paired reasoning items had been pruned/unavailable
```

After this patch, I reran the current-head OpenAI SDK loopback on `1b056f30bd2b8cce4a22631e39d760635083f1fd`. It imported `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` from `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`, sent that exact payload through OpenAI Node SDK 6.39.0 with an in-process `fetch` loopback, and confirmed that both the OpenClaw payload and the serialized SDK `/v1/responses` request omitted the stale signed assistant message id:

```json
{
  "proof": "openai-responses-sdk-loopback",
  "pr_head": "1b056f30bd2b8cce4a22631e39d760635083f1fd",
  "request_path": "/v1/responses",
  "stale_signed_message_id": "msg_real_response_item_requiring_reasoning",
  "stale_id_present_in_openclaw_payload": false,
  "stale_id_present_in_sdk_request": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: signed assistant message ids are no longer replayed when their required reasoning item is absent; signed ids are still replayed when the immediately preceding replayed item is reasoning, and unsigned assistant messages still get the existing synthetic fallback id.
- What was not tested: a live deployed gateway restart or live OpenAI API request with this exact source branch has not been run; this proof refresh revalidated the exact current-head transport payload and SDK serialization path.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): live OpenClaw agent output above captured the OpenAI rejection text and identified the pruned-reasoning replay shape.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the Responses replay path treated persisted assistant text signatures as independently replayable message ids, but OpenAI can require the corresponding reasoning item to be replayed with that message item.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing coverage checked reasoning replay when reasoning was present; it did not check the pruned-reasoning case where only the signed assistant message survived.
- Contributing context (if known): long-lived sessions can prune or omit reasoning items while retaining assistant text signatures.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/openai-responses.reasoning-replay.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a signed assistant message id is omitted when no paired reasoning item was replayed; the same signed id is preserved when paired reasoning was replayed; unsigned text still uses the fallback id.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in request payload construction, before network IO, so the replay-id policy can be tested deterministically.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for the pruned-reasoning signed-message case.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None, except affected OpenAI Responses sessions avoid a provider-side 400 when replay history has a stale signed message id without its paired reasoning item.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[assistant textSignature id survives] -> [reasoning item pruned] -> [request replays message id] -> [OpenAI rejects]

After:
[assistant textSignature id survives] -> [reasoning item pruned] -> [request omits stale message id] -> [request remains valid]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS arm64
- Runtime/container: local Node/Vitest via OpenClaw source checkout
- Model/provider: `openai/gpt-5.4`, OpenAI Responses
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram agent session exposed the failure; source regression covers the transport replay policy.
- Relevant config (redacted): OpenAI Responses API model with reasoning enabled.

### Steps

1. Create a replay candidate with a signed assistant text `message` id.
2. Omit the paired reasoning item, matching a pruned history state.
3. Build the next OpenAI Responses request.

### Expected

- The stale signed assistant message id is omitted when paired reasoning is unavailable.

### Actual

- Before this change, the stale signed assistant message id was replayed even when the paired reasoning item was unavailable.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: current-head real-SDK in-process Responses loopback via an inline `node --import tsx --input-type=module` proof that imported `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` from `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`; whitespace validation via `git diff --check`.
- Edge cases checked: signed id without reasoning is omitted; signed id with reasoning is preserved; unsigned assistant text keeps the synthetic fallback id.
- What you did **not** verify: live gateway deployment on this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: omitting a stale Responses message id may reduce provider-side continuity for that one historical assistant message.
  - Mitigation: the text content and phase metadata are still replayed; the id is omitted only when the paired reasoning item is missing, which is the provider-rejected shape.

target.title: fix(codex): quarantine unsupported dynamic tool schemas
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden Codex dynamic-tool registration so malformed runtime tool schemas are diagnosed and quarantined instead of failing assistant turn startup.
- Validate nested JSON Schema shape hazards that Codex rejects, including malformed schema maps, schema arrays, optional schema-valued keywords, `required`, property dependencies, and dynamic schema keywords.
- Preserve valid current-turn executable tools when only stale registered metadata for the same name is invalid.
- Inline local `$ref` schemas under MCP/tool-schema dependency and additional-item keywords so `$defs` are not stripped while dangling refs remain.
- Keep MCP loopback `tools/list` alive when a tool schema union contains malformed property-schema variants; malformed variants are ignored while later valid variants can still define the property.
- Preserve optionality when MCP loopback union schemas include boolean `true` branches instead of over-tightening merged `required` fields.
- Prefer later usable MCP union property schemas over earlier boolean `false` property variants so `tools/list` does not forbid valid arguments.
- Clean Gemini provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency and conditional branches so inspection and normalization agree.
- Clean shared provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency, conditional, tuple, and additional-item branches so xAI-style inspection and normalization agree.
- Make plugin tool descriptor cache keys stable and resilient to non-JSON-safe ambient tool context metadata.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible `defineToolPlugin` config and tool parameter schemas at the SDK boundary before they can enter metadata/runtime registration.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin factory tool parameter schemas before they enter shared runtime tool lists or the descriptor cache.
- Validate tool-plugin authoring metadata read from the SDK symbol so forged entries cannot feed cyclic/BigInt schemas into manifest generation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema validator inputs before recursive schema walking, cache fingerprinting, or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible bundle-MCP draft-2020-12 schemas before recursive schema walking or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema metadata before recursive normalization or metadata exposure.
- Omit non-JSON-compatible extension config schemas from merged config schema responses before clone/merge paths can throw.
- Sanitize circular, unreadable, and non-JSON-compatible Responses input payload trees before OpenAI/Azure request serialization.
- Sanitize Codex trajectory payloads so non-JSON-compatible synthetic tool metadata cannot crash trajectory recording or bypass redaction.
- Reject unreadable plugin host-hook JSON payloads without throwing from host validation.
- Ignore unreadable Codex MCP elicitation request/display fields without throwing out of approval bridging.
- Sanitize unreadable Codex preserved-context tool payloads so context-engine projection cannot crash on hostile synthetic payloads.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex app-server approval routing, permission-summary, policy-rewrite, and command payloads.
- Tolerate unreadable Codex app-server protocol response/turn fields before schema validation.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server notification correlation fields before routing/logging decisions.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server projector hook routing fields before event projection.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server side-question notification/request fields before side-thread routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server prompt-report schema and notification fields before report hashing or runtime routing decisions.
- Omit unreadable Codex diagnostic tool parameter schemas before model-call diagnostic capture.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server user-input request and resolution fields before chat prompt routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server attempt notification-state params before watchdog and terminal-state decisions.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent notification params/item/content fields before completion extraction.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent task-mirror params/thread/status/item fields before task lifecycle mirroring.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent monitor params/thread/spawn/item fields before child-thread and completion routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server JSON-RPC message params/result fields before request, notification, or pending-response dispatch.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server tool-progress and mirrored-history sanitizer fields before logging or image-payload sanitation.
- Redact Codex app inventory error data through catch-bounded reads so unreadable synthetic fields cannot replace the original refresh failure.
- Fingerprint unreadable, circular, and non-JSON-compatible Codex dynamic tool fields without crashing thread lifecycle decisions.
- Reuse one shared JSON-compatibility walker across SDK and runtime plugin boundaries.
- Keep test fixtures synthetic (`fuzzplugin`/`fuzz_move_*`) instead of naming the reported third-party plugin.
- Move plugin tool metadata storage into a lightweight module so schema diagnostics can name plugin/tool ownership without importing heavier plugin runtime code.
- Out of scope: changing global plugin materialization policy or rejecting valid non-Codex schemas outside the Codex/doctor projection path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

No linked closing issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

No linked related issue.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer-directed hardening/debugging work.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: malformed runtime plugin tool schemas can make Codex reject dynamic tool registration before an assistant turn produces content.
- Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux runners, provider `aws`, using the branch over current `origin/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-tools.schema.test.ts src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`
  - `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`
  - `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/agents/schema/clean-for-gemini.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts`
  - `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/agents/schema/clean-for-xai.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts`

  - `node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Core tool-schema projection tests for malformed nested keywords, valid tuple `items`, valid property dependencies, and synthetic tool names.
- Tool-parameter schema tests for `$ref` in `dependencies` and `additionalItems`.
- Codex dynamic-tool tests for quarantined invalid executable schemas, disabled-call response, and stale registered-only diagnostics that must not hide a valid executable tool.
- Doctor core check fixtures use synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`fuzz_move_*` names.
- MCP loopback regression for malformed union property schemas using synthetic `fuzz_move_delta`.
- MCP loopback regression for boolean union optionality using synthetic `fuzz_optional_delta`.
- MCP loopback regression for boolean `false` property variants using synthetic `fuzz_boolean_delta`.
- Gemini provider compatibility regression for unsupported keywords inside schema-valued dependency branches using synthetic `fuzz_move_delta`.
- xAI-style provider compatibility regression for unsupported keywords inside schema-valued dependency branches using synthetic schema/tool names.
- Plugin descriptor-cache regressions for stable field-order-independent context keys and cycle-safe non-JSON-safe context metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta` names.
- Plugin SDK `defineToolPlugin` regressions for bigint and cyclic parameter schema objects using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `mockplugin` names.
- Runtime plugin materialization regression for non-JSON-safe plugin factory parameter schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta`, while preserving the valid sibling `fuzz_status` tool.
- Tool-plugin authoring metadata regression for forged SDK-symbol metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta` names.
- Plugin config schema-validator regression for cyclic schemas and bigint enum values using synthetic `fuzzplugin` cache keys.
- Bundle-MCP draft-2020-12 schema-validator regression for cyclic schemas and bigint enum values.
- Plugin config schema metadata regressions for cyclic direct/toJSONSchema output and bigint direct schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` cache keys.
- Config schema aggregation regressions for function, symbol, and throwing extension schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` ids.
- Responses image payload sanitizer regressions for circular tool output, circular root input arrays, circular inline image fields, unreadable top-level/nested objects, and non-JSON-compatible primitive payload values using synthetic `fuzzplugin` names.
- Codex trajectory regressions for cyclic, `bigint`, function, symbol, non-finite, redacted, and unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` tool metadata.
- Host-hook JSON validation regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` payload fields.
- Codex elicitation regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing fields and approval display parameter values.
- Codex context projection regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` preserved tool payload fields.
- Codex approval bridge regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing fields, permission-summary values, policy-rewrite payloads, command actions, and command array entries.
- Codex protocol-validator regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` response/turn fields before schema validation.
- Codex notification-correlation regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing and nested turn fields before active-run routing/logging decisions.
- Codex event-projector regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` hook routing fields before hook event projection.
- Codex side-question regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` completion, user-input routing, and retry/error fields before side-thread routing.
- Codex attempt-context regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool schema fields before prompt-report stats and stable schema hashing.
- Codex attempt-diagnostics regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool parameter schema fields before model-call diagnostic capture.
- Codex user-input bridge regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` request and resolution fields before chat prompt routing.
- Codex attempt notification-state regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` notification params before watchdog and terminal-state decisions.
- Codex native subagent notification regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, item, and content fields before completion extraction.
- Codex native subagent task-mirror regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, thread, status, item, and agent-state fields before task lifecycle mirroring.
- Codex native subagent monitor regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, thread, spawn source, item receiver-thread, and agent-state fields before monitor routing.
- Codex app-server client regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` JSON-RPC message fields before dispatch, plus empty-message JSON-RPC error preservation.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Codex dynamic-tool registration could reject malformed active tool schemas before content was produced.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Malformed Codex dynamic tools are now disabled instead of being advertised or crashing registration.

What is the highest-risk area?

Overly strict schema-shape validation could disable a valid runtime tool schema.

How is that risk mitigated?

The projection preserves valid JSON Schema forms covered by review, including boolean schemas, tuple `items`, `dependencies` string arrays, and schema-valued `additionalItems`; focused tests and changed-gate proof cover the touched surfaces.

The PR branch adds opt-in Telegram interleaved progress rendering, two Telegram preview config flags, docs/UI metadata, generated config metadata, focused helper tests, and a small plugin-SDK type cleanup.

commands.author: anagnorisis2peripeteia

target.title: feat(telegram): opt-in interleaved progress lane
target.body:
Add an opt-in Telegram progress projection that renders already-authorized structured runtime events into one durable live message, matching CLI ergonomics without changing execution semantics or exposing new data by default.

## What this does

When `channels.telegram.streaming.preview.interleavedProgress` is enabled, reasoning text and the existing structured runtime events are rendered into a **single durable live message** on the reasoning lane (Codex/Claude-CLI style), instead of the separate tool-progress lane.

It consumes the **existing** agent-event callbacks with **no contract change** — `onReasoningStream`, `onToolStart`, `onItemEvent`, `onPlanUpdate`, `onApprovalEvent`, `onCommandOutput`, `onPatchSummary` — and falls back to the current `pushStreamToolProgress` lane whenever the flag is off.

## Scope boundary

Projection-only. It does **not** change prompts, model routing, tool execution, auth, or default visibility — it only changes Telegram rendering, and only when explicitly enabled.

- **Default off.** Gated on preview tool progress already being enabled **and** an explicit opt-in **and** a reasoning lane existing, so it inherits every existing group/DM/visibility gate and cannot expose anything the tool-progress lane wasn't already authorized to show.
- **No raw data.** Tool lines show the tool name only (never raw args); item/plan/approval/command/patch lines show the title only (never raw command output); every line is whitespace-collapsed and length-capped.
- **No new event contract** (Pass 1). The shared structured-event contract already exists in `src/infra/agent-events.ts`; this PR only adds a Telegram renderer over it. Extracting/normalizing a shared contract is deliberately deferred to a follow-up once the UX is proven.

## Changes

- `src/config/types.telegram.ts` + `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`: add the opt-in `streaming.preview.interleavedProgress` flag.
- `extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.ts`: pure render helpers (gate, sanitize/cap, header-strip, message render, reasoning checkpoint, status-line append).
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts`: wire the 7 callbacks through the renderer with fallback; clear the rolling timer in the run's `finally`.

## Tests

`extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.test.ts` (18 cases) covers:
- disabled-by-default, explicit opt-out, tool-progress-off, and no-reasoning-lane (room-event / source-delivery-suppressed) all leave the gate off → current behaviour preserved, no progress leak.
- single `Thinking` header (no duplication); body stored header-stripped.
- rolling-timer suffix present while running, dropped (shorter strict-prefix) once cleared so a full-text lane update replaces stale timer text instead of appending.
- whitespace-collapse + length cap so no large/secret-looking value floods a line.
- reasoning checkpoint appends only newly-arrived text on cumulative re-delivery.

## Not yet covered

- End-to-end integration through the dispatch harness (timer-interval clearing on every exit path is wired in the run's `finally`; 429/backoff uses `stream.update` not forced flush — both verified by construction/reading, not yet by a harness test).
- Live current-head Telegram capture.

target.title: fix(memory-core): cap Dreaming short-term recall growth
target.body:
## Summary

- cap memory-core Dreaming short-term recall retention at 512 entries
- truncate stored recall snippets to 800 chars during normal recording and legacy normalization
- surface over-limit recall stores in audit/doctor repair output and report overflow removals

Fixes #87095

## Real behavior proof

behavior: Dreaming `short-term-recall.json` no longer grows without bound during normal recall recording, and an existing oversized recall store is repaired down to the same cap.

environment: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, Node 22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0, real temporary workspaces under `/tmp`, real filesystem JSON writes.

steps:

1. Created a temporary workspace and recorded 517 short-term recall results through `recordShortTermRecalls`.
2. Read the persisted `memory/.dreams/short-term-recall.json`.
3. Created a second temporary workspace with a legacy 515-entry `short-term-recall.json`.
4. Ran `auditShortTermPromotionArtifacts` and `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`.
5. Read the repaired store from disk.

evidence:

```json
{
  "workspaceRoot": "/tmp/openclaw-recall-proof-yhNQXz",
  "normalRecord": {
    "maxEntries": 512,
    "beforeAttempted": 517,
    "afterEntries": 512,
    "maxSnippetLength": 800,
    "droppedOldest": true,
    "keptNewest": true
  },
  "repair": {
    "beforeEntries": 515,
    "issueCodes": [
      "recall-store-over-limit"
    ],
    "removedOverflowEntries": 3,
    "afterEntries": 512,
    "maxSnippetLength": 800,
    "droppedOldest": true
  }
}
```

observedResult: Normal recording attempted 517 recall entries and persisted 512. Legacy repair started from 515 entries, reported `recall-store-over-limit`, removed 3 overflow entries, and persisted 512 entries. Both paths capped stored snippets at 800 chars.

notTested: Full live Dreaming cron plus `/new` session-context E2E was not run locally. The focused proof covers the real memory-core recall store write/repair paths on filesystem workspaces; regression tests cover normal recording, repair/audit behavior, and doctor summary consumers.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve Matrix room-id case in session keys (#75670)
target.body:
Fixes #75670.

## Summary

Matrix room IDs (and thread event IDs) are opaque and **case-sensitive** per the Matrix spec, but OpenClaw's session-key canonicalization lowercased them. That forked one room into duplicate sessions and produced `403 M_FORBIDDEN` on recovery/identity paths that reconstruct the target from the (lowercased) session key — even though `deliveryContext.to` stayed correct. This is the **session-key layer** of #75670 (the issue's thread-event symptom was already handled by an injected identity thread-normalizer; the room-ID/session-key layer was not).

Instead of a Matrix-specific patch, this adds a **generic, opt-in case-preservation registry** that defaults to today's lowercase behavior and lets channels enroll individually. Signal group preservation (#82853) is encoded to match its prior behavior exactly; Matrix `channel`/`group` is the newly-enrolled channel. Every un-enrolled channel is byte-for-byte unchanged.

## Regression history

- **2026-04-19** internal note: Matrix room IDs are exact literals and must not be case-normalized.
- **#78206** (closed) → **#77205** (merged, `ceca7fdf`) fixed the lowercased room ID on the **direct send** path.
- The bug kept recurring on the **session-key/recovery** path (delivery metadata was correct, but lookup/recovery/identity rebuilt the target from the lowercased key): observed `403 User … not in room <lowercased>` repeatedly (e.g. 2026-05-23, 2026-05-26 across two rooms), which is the live recurrence tracked in #75670.
- **#57337** (closed, unmerged) was an earlier attempt at "preserve case in group/channel peer IDs for session keys" — revisited here as a generic registry with read-time precedence and live proof.

## Related / prior work

- **#82853** (merged) — Signal group session-id preservation; generalized here into the shared registry.
- **#63828 / #64343** (merged) — Matrix ACP thread-binding targets; same opaque-id-case class, different surface.
- Cross-channel evidence motivating the *generic* (not Matrix-only) design: **#81704** (LINE recipients), **#75261 / #71798** (cron Matrix room IDs), **#82141 / #75303** (cron LINE/channel casing).
- No currently-open PR overlaps this session-key surface.

## What changes

- **`CASE_PRESERVING_PEERS` registry + `isCasePreservingPeer(channel, peerKind)`** (`session-key-utils.ts`) — single policy source. `signal/group` = `segment` span (unscoped, behavior-identical to the old regex); `matrix/channel|group` = `tail` span (opaque room id incl. embedded `:server`, plus any `:thread:<event>` suffix).
- Consulted at the three sites that lowercased the room id:
  - construction — `normalizeSessionPeerId`
  - store canonicalization — `normalizeSessionKeyPreservingOpaquePeerIds` (rewritten to collect preserved spans and lowercase only the complement; tail matcher anchored to the agent-scoped head)
  - gateway send — explicit `request.sessionKey` now uses the opaque-preserving normalizer instead of raw lowercase
- **Exact mixed-case wins over folded legacy** in `resolveSessionStoreEntry` and `delivery-info.ts` lookup (exact ranks below routability, above freshness). Legacy lowercased rows collapse toward the exact key on the next write.
- **Case-distinct safety** (`store-entry.ts`): a folded lowercase key is treated as a collapsible/returnable legacy alias **only when its delivery target confirms it is this room's lowercased artifact** (not a genuinely case-distinct sibling room). Distinct rooms are never deleted or cross-read. (See "Codex review findings" below.)

## Compatibility expectations

- **Signal** group preservation unchanged (scoped + unscoped `Signal:Group:<id>`; thread suffix still lowercased).
- **Non-Matrix channels** (Telegram, Slack, LINE, …) — keys lowercased exactly as before; a non-Matrix peer id literally containing `:thread:` is unaffected.
- **Matrix DM / MXID** — out of scope by decision; stays lowercased.
- Migration is fallback + write-time-collapse, **not** a bulk rewrite of stored session files.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Matrix room IDs were lowercased into session keys (`agent:<a>:matrix:channel:<lowercased-room-id>`), forking sessions and causing `403 M_FORBIDDEN` on send/recovery paths that rebuild the target from the key, even though the bot is joined to the real mixed-case room.
- **Real environment tested:** A long-running production OpenClaw gateway (self-hosted Matrix homeserver, multi-account, macOS) running this exact patch on top of current `main`. The gateway was reloaded onto the patched build (PID confirmed on the new dist); two rooms that had logged the bug were exercised with live traffic. Room IDs / homeserver / MXID partially masked below; ellipses preserve the real opaque bytes' case.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** (1) `pnpm build` then reload the LaunchDaemon; (2) confirm the running process is on the patched dist; (3) let organic traffic hit room A and send a fresh human message into room B; (4) inspect the live session store (`~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/sessions/sessions.json`) and the gateway delivery logs for both rooms; (5) run the existing matrix-403 watcher.
- **Evidence after fix (copied live output, partially masked):**
  - **Before (old build), the recurring failure** — `gateway.err.log`:
    ```
    2026-05-26T10:56:48 [tools] message failed: MatrixError: [403] User @m…ry:<server> not in room !trpa…zeflu:<server>
    2026-05-26T10:32:08 [tools] message failed: MatrixError: [403] User @m…ry:<server> not in room !stmc…cqvxy:<server>
    ```
    (note the **lowercased** room id in the failing send target).
  - **After (patched build), room B (sage room) — same send now succeeds** — `gateway.log`:
    ```
    2026-05-27T13:14:58 setting pendingEvent status to sent in !trPa…zEflU:<server>
      event ID ~!trPa…zEflU:<server>:m1779902098492.0 -> $N91n…di8o
    ```
    delivered to the **mixed-case** room with a real Matrix event id; and the new persisted channel session key is mixed-case `agent:<a>:matrix:channel:!trPa…zEflU:<server>` (a single key, no fork).
  - **Room A (dev room)** — persisted session key for the live routable session is now mixed-case `agent:<a>:matrix:channel:!StMc…CqvXy:<server>` with `deliveryContext.to = room:!StMc…CqvXy:<server>`.
  - **Zero** case-`403`s on either room since the reload; the matrix-403 watcher reports **0 case-bug events** in the post-reload window.
- **Observed result after fix:** outbound sends to mixed-case Matrix rooms succeed where they previously `403`'d; lookup/recovery/identity see the correct mixed-case key. Non-Matrix channels and Signal behave exactly as before.
- **What was not tested:**
  - The documented `main:thread:<event>` residual (a thread key with no `<channel>:<peerKind>:` boundary still lowercases the event id — the main-session thread shape, not the room-session shape behind this issue).
  - Windows/Linux runtime (logic is platform-independent).
  - **Separate surface (not this PR):** some cron jobs carry pre-fix lowercased `sessionTarget`/`sessionKey` in their stored config. On fire they replay the old lowercased key (bypassing the now-fixed *construction* path) and materialize a **non-routable, hollow** session record (no `deliveryContext`, no origin) — which causes **no `403`** (the routable session is the mixed-case one). This is stale cron-config data, overlapping #75261, and is corrected by a data migration of the cron configs, not by this PR's code.
  - `pnpm check` passes except a pre-existing, unrelated `npm shrinkwrap guard` failure (AWS-SDK lockfile/node_modules version skew) — this PR touches no `package.json`/lockfile.

## Test plan

- `pnpm build` — clean.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean.
- `pnpm vitest run src/sessions/session-key-case-preservation.test.ts src/config/sessions/delivery-info.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` — 314 passed.
- New `src/sessions/session-key-case-preservation.test.ts` regression matrix:
  - registry membership (Matrix channel/group + Signal group enrolled; DM / non-Matrix not);
  - construction + store-canon preserve Matrix room id (incl. `:server`) and `:thread:<event>` suffix;
  - case-distinct rooms never collapse to one key;
  - Signal scoped + unscoped preserved, Signal thread suffix lowercased, segment whitespace trimmed (legacy parity);
  - non-Matrix / `:thread:`-shaped peer id / Matrix DM all stay lowercased;
  - `resolveSessionStoreEntry`: case-distinct sibling neither deleted nor returned as `existing`; confirmed lowercased artifact still collapses + reads;
  - documented `main:thread` residual.
- Local `codex review --base origin/main`: AGREE.

## Acceptance criteria (from the #75670 handoff)

- [x] Matrix room IDs preserved exactly in new session keys.
- [x] Matrix thread event IDs preserved (room-scoped path).
- [x] Store lookup / delivery do not re-lowercase the `:matrix:channel:<room-id>` suffix.
- [x] Legacy lowercased entries treated as aliases (exact mixed-case wins) without deleting case-distinct rooms.
- [x] Regression tests cover mixed-case room, thread event, delivery metadata, no duplicate/cross-contaminated rows, and non-Matrix behavior unchanged.

## Codex review findings (addressed)

- **P2 — "Do not delete case-distinct Matrix sessions"** (codex-connector, inline on `store-entry.ts`): the original folded-legacy collapse could delete (and read-cross-contaminate) a genuinely case-distinct sibling room. **Resolved**: the folded lowercase key is materialized as a legacy alias for both delete and read **only when `isLowercasedLegacyAlias(...)` confirms it delivers to this room's real mixed-case id**; the loop now collapses only true canonical aliases. Three+ new tests cover it. Local codex re-review: AGREE, P2 fully resolved.

## AI-assisted disclosure 🤖

AI-assisted (Claude). Developed and reviewed via an iterative local `codex review` loop (multiple plan rounds + diff reviews, ending AGREE, including the P2 follow-up above). The real-behavior proof is from a human-operated production gateway, not generated. Plan/review notes available on request.

## Notes for review

- Touches session-state + message-delivery (hence the `merge-risk` labels) — the case-distinct safety logic and the registry default-lowercase make the blast radius opt-in and non-Matrix-neutral.
- The fix is currently deployed on the author's production gateway ahead of merge (local patch), which produced the proof above; it drops on rebase once this lands.
- **Allow edits by maintainers** is enabled (takeover-ready).
